coredata.dc:title: Selective determination of Fe (III) with carbon dots as photoluminescence Probes: Chemometric analysis using Excitation-Emission matrices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Castro, Rafael C.
N.M.J. Páscoa, Ricardo
Lúcia M. F. S. Saraiva, M.
Santos, João L.M.
Ribeiro, David S.M.
coredata.dc:description: The selective and accurate determination of metal ions, crucial in various physiological processes within the human body, is of paramount importance, particularly when these ions, such as Fe(III), coexist with others in the sample. The manuscript addresses the challenge of determining Fe(III) selectively in the presence of interfering species. To achieve this, carbon dots (CDs) are employed as photoluminescence probes due to their biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. The chemometric analysis of photoluminescence (PL) data was used to overcome selectivity challenges associated with CDs. This study utilized fluorescence excitation-emission matrices (EEMs), considered as second-order data, and applied unfolded partial least squares followed by residual bilinearization (U-PLS/RBL) to account for uncalibrated species. The developed model methodology was assessed using spiked samples containing uncalibrated and interfering species, namely Al(III), Pb(II) and Ni(II), at different concentration levels. The achieved limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) at 0.02 and 0.06 mmol/L, respectively, highlight the high sensitivity of the approach. Notably, the application of one RBL component significantly reduces RMSEP values in samples spiked individually with each uncalibrated and interfering species, demonstrating the methodology’s effectiveness in overcoming strong interactions with CDs. Interestingly, this reduction was not observed in test samples containing only Fe(III), where optimal results were achieved without the application of the RBL component. This study introduces a novel approach by formulating a selective methodology for the determination of Fe(III) in the presence of interfering species, employing carbon dots (CDs) as photoluminescence probes. The application of chemometric analysis, specifically U-PLS/RBL, to mitigate selectivity challenges, coupled with the demonstrated sensitivity characterized by low LOD and LOQ values, emphasizes the scientific significance of this research. The results highlight the practical viability of the methodology, particularly in complex samples, contributing valuable insights to the field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Dots
Chemometrics
Excitation-Emission Matrix
U-PLS/RBL
Metal ions
Second-order data

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical features of the synthesized CDs
Reactivity of N-CDs in the presence of distinct metal ions
PL response of CDs to Fe3+, Al3+, Pb2+ and Ni2+
Mechanism of the CDs PL quenching effect by Fe3+ and the other cations
Fe3+ determination using EEM and chemometric analysis
Stability evaluation of CDs through EEM data analysis
Multivariate calibration of Fe3+ using EEM data
Selective determination of Fe3+ in the absence and presence of each interfering species using RBL method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–Visible absorption spectrum of CDs. b) PL emission spectra of CDs solution (λem ranging from 400 to 550 nm) at different excitation wavelengths (λex ranging from 320 to 390 nm with increments of 2 nm). c) PL emission spectrum of CDs excited at 356 nm. d) PL decay curve of CDs (λem = 436 nm).
A) PL spectra of CDs in the absence and presence of various metal ions. b) PL responses of as-prepared CDs to individual metal ions at the concentration of 0.8 mmol/L.
PL emission spectra of CDs sensing probe without and with a) Fe3+, b) Pb2+, c) Al3+, and d) Ni2+ at different concentrations.
Stern–Volmer plots displaying the ratio of the PL intensities (●) and PL lifetimes ratio (■) as a function of Fe3+ concentrations.
EEM of CDs: a) in the absence and b) in the presence of Fe3+ at a concentration of 0.20 mmol/L.
A typical calibration curve for Fe3+ standards obtained through U-PLS using 2 LV.
Calibration curves for Pb2+ (a), Al3+ (b) and Ni2+ (c) standards obtained through U-PLS.
Results for the prediction of Fe3+ in samples with and without the presence of Pb2+ as an uncalibrated species.
Results for the prediction of Fe3+ in samples with and without the presence of Al3+ as an uncalibrated species.
Results for the prediction of Fe3+ in samples with and without the presence of Ni2+ as an uncalibrated species.

head.ce:title: Selective determination of Fe (III) with carbon dots as photoluminescence Probes: Chemometric analysis using Excitation-Emission matrices

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: LAQV, REQUIMTE, Department of Chemical Sciences, Laboratory of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Porto, Rua de Jorge Viterbo Ferreira n° 228, 4050-313 Porto, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Rua de Jorge Viterbo Ferreira n° 228

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: LAQV, REQUIMTE, Department of Chemical Sciences, Laboratory of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Porto, Rua de Jorge Viterbo Ferreira n° 228, 4050-313 Porto, Portugal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The selective and accurate determination of metal ions, crucial in various physiological processes within the human body, is of paramount importance, particularly when these ions, such as Fe(III), coexist with others in the sample. The manuscript addresses the challenge of determining Fe(III) selectively in the presence of interfering species. To achieve this, carbon dots (CDs) are employed as photoluminescence probes due to their biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. The chemometric analysis of photoluminescence (PL) data was used to overcome selectivity challenges associated with CDs. This study utilized fluorescence excitation-emission matrices (EEMs), considered as second-order data, and applied unfolded partial least squares followed by residual bilinearization (U-PLS/RBL) to account for uncalibrated species.

The developed model methodology was assessed using spiked samples containing uncalibrated and interfering species, namely Al(III), Pb(II) and Ni(II), at different concentration levels. The achieved limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) at 0.02 and 0.06 mmol/L, respectively, highlight the high sensitivity of the approach. Notably, the application of one RBL component significantly reduces RMSEP values in samples spiked individually with each uncalibrated and interfering species, demonstrating the methodology’s effectiveness in overcoming strong interactions with CDs. Interestingly, this reduction was not observed in test samples containing only Fe(III), where optimal results were achieved without the application of the RBL component.

This study introduces a novel approach by formulating a selective methodology for the determination of Fe(III) in the presence of interfering species, employing carbon dots (CDs) as photoluminescence probes. The application of chemometric analysis, specifically U-PLS/RBL, to mitigate selectivity challenges, coupled with the demonstrated sensitivity characterized by low LOD and LOQ values, emphasizes the scientific significance of this research. The results highlight the practical viability of the methodology, particularly in complex samples, contributing valuable insights to the field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions play vital roles in various physiological processes within the human body; however, their deficiency or excess can result in severe pathological conditions [1]. Among these metal ions, iron, which exists in ferrous (II) and ferric (III) ionic states, is a fundamental trace element for human metabolism since it serves as a metabolism mediator, oxygen transporter, transcriptional gene regulator, and electron carrier [2,3]. When comparing the two oxidation states, Fe (III) has a greater tendency to generate reactive oxygen species, promoting oxidative stress and causing cellular damage [2]. So, the excessive presence of Fe (III) can deteriorate biological systems through a catalytic mechanism contributing to diseases such as Parkinson's syndrome, Alzheimer's disease, and cancer [3].

In environmental, biological, or food samples, various metal ions can coexist. Therefore, it is crucial to employ suitable analytical methods capable of distinguishing between them and accurately quantifying the one of interest. Several analytical methodologies, including spectrophotometry, atomic absorption spectrophotometry (AAS), inductively coupled plasma- optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and cathodic or anodic stripping voltammetry have traditionally been used to determine Fe(III) in various samples [3–5]. Despite their widespread application, these traditional analytical methods exhibit several shortcomings such as susceptibility to matrix interferences and time-consuming and labour-intensive sample preparation, and some of them result in the destruction of the sample during analysis. Additionally, they often require expensive and sophisticated instrumentation and skilled operators [3,4]. Conversely, optical methods, especially those employing fluorescent probes, can offer distinct advantages for Fe(III) determination [6,7]. These methods provide high sensitivity, enabling the detection of trace concentrations crucial for applications in environmental monitoring and biological studies. Furthermore, the reduced interference from background signals, improved signal-to-noise ratios, and ease of operation make optical methods efficient and valuable tools for comprehensive Fe(III) determination across various fields [6,7].

Nanomaterials have emerged as powerful analytical tools in optical methodologies, providing unprecedented capabilities for the sensitive and reliable determination of metal ions [6,8,9]. Among these nanomaterials, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [10] and carbon dots (CDs) [11] have garnered significant attention, especially as photoluminescence (PL) probes, due to their impressive optical, electronic, and surface properties. While both CDs and QDs are convenient nanomaterials, in selected applications utilizing CDs for the determination of metal ions brings about specific benefits. Indeed, CDs are an important class of sustainable and biocompatible PL sensing platforms which have low toxicity and an environmentally benign nature [1]. These features are advantageous when working with biological samples such as living cells, tissues, or physiological fluids, thus paving the way for potential applications in metal ions sensing within biological environments [12]. Additionally, they can be obtained from green and sustainable carbon sources, contributing to their environmentally friendly profile and for sustainable nanomaterial production [13]. The simplicity of synthesis, usually involving the carbonization of organic precursors, contributes to cost-effectiveness and scalability, rendering CDs more accessible for large-scale production when compared to quantum dots [14,15]. In addition to the abovementioned features, CDs exhibit a notable ability for metal ion sensing and for that reason, they have been used for the sensitive determination of a wide range of metal ions in different kinds of samples. This is attributed to the presence of surface oxygen moieties such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which contribute to the coordination and chelation of metal ions, leading to specific interactions that result in the modulation of CDs' optical properties, usually PL quenching [2]. Despite the interesting characteristics for the determination of metal ions, CDs’ selectivity has been identified as one of the main challenges [16]. These nanomaterials may exhibit similar PL responses to multiple metal ions, leading to cross-reactivity [16]. Without guaranteeing selectivity, CD-based sensing elements may not be able to distinguish between different metal ions, resulting in inaccurate detection and quantification of specific metal ions in the presence of other ions or compounds that may be present in the sample matrix. Bearing this in mind, the researchers have explored innovative strategies and technologies seeking the enhancement of the selectivity of CD-based sensing platforms for metal ion determination [5]. These efforts involve surface functionalization of CDs with specific ligands or receptors [17], optimization of experimental conditions, and the use of chemometric methods for data analysis [18,19]. Among these options, the use of chemometric models for data analysis provides a simpler, faster and cheaper approach to enhance the selectivity of CD-based methodologies for metal ion sensing. Chemometrics involves the application of statistical and mathematical methods to analyse complex chemical data allowing the extraction of meaningful information which can help discern patterns and correlations in the PL data generated by CDs in the presence of metal ions [19–21]. This is important to distinguish the signal of interest from background noise or interference, contributing to the robustness of the sensing platform [22]. In multivariate analyses, the quantity of data obtained per sample significantly enhances the performance of chemometric models [23]. This improvement not only facilitates a more robust calibration for quantitative assessments but also ensures accurate predictions of concentrations. As a result, reliable quantification of metal ions in samples with complex matrices becomes possible [23]. To achieve this, the acquisition of second-order data, such as Excitation-Emission Matrices (EEMs), is crucial, playing a key role in enhancing the selectivity, accuracy, and overall performance of chemometric models, particularly in complex sample matrices [22]. So, the second-order advantage, which allows the quantification of the target metal ion even in the presence of uncalibrated interferents, can be obtained by applying suitable chemometric methods namely Residual Bilinearization (RBL) [24,25]. RBL is a post-calibration procedure that complements multi-way chemometric models, such as unfolded partial least-squares (U-PLS) and N-way PLS (N-PLS), enabling the identification of patterns or relationships in the data that might not be adequately captured by the initial model [26]. Its purpose is to model the residues (differences between predicted and observed values) of the U-PLS or N-PLS for a test sample as a combination of bilinear contributions from unexpected components [26].

This work aimed to develop an analytical methodology for the determination of Fe(III) using CDs as a fluorescence probe. The quantification of Fe(III) was assessed both in the absence and presence of individual interferents, namely Al(III), Ni(II), and Pb(II). In the presence of Fe(III), the CDs PL was inhibited, as observed for Al(III) and Pb(II). In contrast, Ni(II) induced an enhancement of the optical properties of the carbon-based nanoparticle. EEMs, representing second-order data, were acquired and analysed using U-PLS without and with a post-calibration step of residual bilinearization (RBL). Thus, the importance of analysing EEM data by U-PLS/RBL is intended to be demonstrated for the selective and accurate determination of Fe(III) in the presence of interfering species without the need to include them in the calibration step.

All solutions and standards utilized in this study were meticulously prepared using analytical reagent-grade chemicals, without undergoing any pre-treatment. They were prepared with water purified through a Milli-Q system, guaranteeing a conductivity level not exceeding 0.1 µS cm−1.

The reagents, including ferric chloride (FeCl3, 97 %), ferrous sulphate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O, ≥99.0 %), nickel chloride (NiCl2, 99 %), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, ≥98 %), Calcium chloride (CaCl2, ≥97 %), manganese sulphate monohydrate (MnSO4·H2O, ≥98 %), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, 98 %), and silver nitrate (AgNO3, >99 %), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich® (St. Louis, MO, USA). The 1000 mg L-1 standard solutions of lead nitrate (traceable to SRM from NIST, Pb(NO3)2 in 0.5 HNO3), aluminium nitrate (traceable to SRM from NIST, Al(NO3)3 in 0.5 HNO3), and copper nitrate (traceable to SRM from NIST Cu(NO3)2, in 0.5 HNO3), were purchased from Merck® (Darmstadt, Germany). To synthesize CDs, citric acid (C6H8O7, ≥99.5 %) and ethylenediamine (N2C2H6, ≥99.5 %) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich® (St. Louis, MO, USA).

A 0.8 mmol/L Fe3+ intermediate solution was prepared by dissolving 6.49 mg of FeCl3 in 50 mL of 0.1 mol/L HNO3. For the preparation of 0.8 mmol/L intermediate solutions of Ag+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, a similar procedure was carried out by dissolving a proper amount of the respective powder in 50 mL of 0.1 mol/L HNO3. The 0.8 mmol/L of Al3+, Pb2+ and Cu2+ were prepared by proper dilution from the respective standard solution in 50 mL of 0.1 mol/L HNO3.
Excitation-Emission matrices were recorded at room temperature employing a PerkinElmer FL-8500 spectrofluorometer (Waltham, MA, USA).
The Quantaurus QY C11347-11 spectrometer (Hamamatsu, Japan) equipped with an integrating sphere was employed to measure the absolute Photoluminescence Quantum Yields (PL QY).

The photoluminescence lifetime of CDs was assessed in the presence and absence of ions using a DeltaFlexTM TCSPC Lifetime spectrofluorometer from Horiba Scientific, Kyoto, Japan. This system is equipped with a NanoLED pulsed light source, DeltaHub timing electronics, and PPD picosecond detection modules.
pH measurements were conducted using a sensION+pH31 GLP Laboratory pH meter.

The synthesis of CDs followed the methodology outlined in our previous works [27–29]. In summary, 0.5 g of citric acid and 0.3 mL of ethylenediamine were mixed in deionized water to achieve a final concentration of 10 % (m/v) citric acid. To achieve a pH of 3.8 in the mixture, an appropriate volume of 1 mol/L HCl solution was added. Subsequently, the resulting solution was heated to 260 °C for 5 h in a 40 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. After the synthesis reaction, the solution was allowed to reach room temperature. Finally, the solution underwent dialysis against Milli-Q water for 5 days, using a dialysis membrane (Spectra/Por 6–1000 MWCO, Spectrum Labs™).
For executing the EEM assay, the intermediate CDs solution was prepared by diluting 20 μL of the crude solution with ultrapure water, resulting in a final volume of 30 mL at a ratio of 1:1500.

For the calibration step, the assay involved the addition of the required amounts of 0.1 molL-1 HNO3 solution and 0.8 mmol/L Fe3+ intermediary solution into Eppendorf tubes. Subsequently, 50 μL of the intermediate CDs solution was added to make up the volume to 2 mL. Following the addition of the PL probe, the resulting mixture was shaken and poured into a 10-mm optical path quartz cell for EEM acquisition.

In the case of spiked samples, a similar process was followed. In 2 mL Eppendorf tubes appropriate amounts of 0.1 molL-1 HNO3 solution, 0.8 mmol/L Fe3+ intermediary solution, 0.8 mmolL-1 intermediary solution of the interfering ion (Al3+, Pb2+ or Ni2+, individually) and 50 μL of the intermediate CDs solution were sequentially added, shaken and transferred to a quartz cell.

EEMs were acquired in an excitation wavelength ranging from 320 to 390 nm (excitation increment of 2 nm), and in an emission wavelength ranging from 400 to 550 nm (emission increment of 0.1 nm) using both emission and excitation slit widths at 5 nm.

The selected chemometric model for data analysis, specifically for quantifying Fe3+, was unfolded partial least squares (U-PLS), followed by residual bilinearization (RBL) if interfering species are present [30]. Since EEMs data can be considered as second-order data, it is possible to achieve the second-order advantage.

The EEMs data can be considered second-order data due to its three-dimensional structure (X×Y×Z), where X represents the excitation wavelength, Y represents the emission wavelength and Z represents the number of samples. The application of U-PLS starts with refolding the three-dimensional data into a XY×Z matrix, and then each sample vector is modelled against the concentration of Fe3+. During this calibration step, only information related to the concentration of Fe3+ is provided.

The assessment of the optimal number of latent variables (LV) was performed following Haaland and Tomas criterion, using the leave-one-out cross-validation method [31]. Accordingly, this criterion states that the optimal number of latent variables should be selected when the prediction error sum of squares (PRESS), expressing the fitness of the developed PLS model against the estimated concentrations, is not statistically different between consecutive latent variables. Specifically, if the F-ratio probability of a latent variable is below 0.75, it is considered optimal.

After determining the optimal number of latent variables, it is time to project the validation samples into the calibration model. The application of RBL depends on the presence of uncalibrated species. When present, the RBL procedure is adopted to ensure accurate quantification of these samples. Relying on singular value decomposition, RBL minimises the residual errors of the samples with uncalibrated species to values similar to the noise of the instrument used.

The application of U-PLS/RBL was carried out using the MVC2 interface (download available on [32]), which allowed to obtain the figures of merit following the equations given in [33]. To learn more about U-PLS and RBL, readers are advised to consult the following references [24,34].

The EEMs data were collected between 320 to 388 nm every 2 nm intervals for excitation and within 400 to 550 nm at 0.1 nm intervals for emission. For data analysis, only the range between 330 and 370 nm for excitation and 420 to 470 nm for emission was considered to avoid the influence of Rayleigh scattering on the fluorescence signal, resulting in a total of 10,521 data points (21 x 501) modelled for each sample. Prior to U-PLS/RBL modelling, the EEM data were mean centered.
Data analysis was performed under Matlab environment (version 8.6, MathWorks, Natick, USA) using the MVC2 routine [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical features of the synthesized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of as-prepared CDs were investigated through UV–vis spectrophotometry, time-resolved and steady-state fluorimetry, and quantum yield (QY). The UV–visible spectrum of the synthesized QDs depicted in Fig. 1 a shows a typical absorption band between 320 and 390 nm with a maximum centered at 342 nm. This peak corresponds to the n–π∗ transition of C=O bond [35].

The fluorescence spectra were recorded while varying the excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 390 nm in 2 nm increments. The fluorescence spectra depicted in Fig. 1b demonstrate a noticeable dependence on the excitation wavelength, wherein the maximum emission wavelengths were red-shifted (bathochromic effect) from 434 to 470 nm while increasing the excitation wavelength. The intensity of the emission band initially increased and then significantly decreased as the excitation shifted towards longer wavelengths. The maximum intensity of fluorescence emission occurred at 436 nm when excited at 356 nm (Fig. 1c). This suggests that the CDs' emission is regulated by surface states, and the variation in emission centers on the surface leads to excitation-dependent emission [36,37]. The PL QY of the synthesized CDs was 19.5 ± 0.2 % measured with an integrating sphere and fixing the excitation wavelength at 356 nm.

The PL dynamic properties of CDs were meticulously investigated to ascertain both radiative and non-radiative relaxation rates. The results presented in Fig. 1d reveal a multiexponential emission decay, with the acquired data fitted using a three-component exponential-decay function. Additionally, a low value of chi-squared (χ2 = 1.08) and the absence of significant deviations in the residuals were confirmed. Based on the observations, it can be concluded that the PL emission of CDs arises from three different radiative processes: a short-lived component (τ = 1.51 ± 0.02 ns), a medium-lived component (τ = 5.5 ± 0.1 ns), and a long-lived component (τ = 11.36 ± 0.09 ns). The average lifetime, calculated following the equation described by Sillen et al. [38], was determined to be 8.0 ± 0.1 ns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactivity of N-CDs in the presence of distinct metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The reactivity of the synthesised CDs was evaluated in the presence of various metal ions. With this purpose the PL properties of CDs solutions, obtained from a 1:60000 dilution of the crude solution, were monitored in both the absence and presence of 0.80 mmol/L concentrations of individual metal ions, namely Ag+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ and Al3+.

The pH of iron (III) solutions is critical because it directly influences the speciation and solubility of this ion. In low pH conditions (acidic conditions), iron (III) predominantly exists as the hydrated ion Fe3+. As the pH increases from slightly acidic conditions to neutral conditions, hydroxo complexes such as Fe(OH)2+ and Fe(OH)2 + begin to form. As the pH continues to rise to neutral and slightly basic conditions, insoluble iron (III) hydroxide, Fe(OH)3, is formed and precipitates out of the solution [39,40].

The solubility of iron (III) is also highly pH-dependent. At low pH, iron (III) remains soluble because the Fe3+ ions are stable in highly acidic conditions. As the pH increases, iron (III) starts to hydrolyse and form hydroxo complexes. At a certain pH (typically around pH 3–4), Fe(OH)3 begins to precipitate. At high pH (alkaline conditions), iron(III) hydroxide becomes increasingly insoluble and precipitates completely [39,40].

Therefore, maintain the iron (III) ion in its desired Fe3+ form, it is essential to control the pH of the solution accurately. Given the importance of pH in the stability and speciation of iron (III) ions, all solutions were prepared using a 0.1 mol/L nitric acid (HNO3) solution. This approach was chosen to ensure a consistent acidic environment, thereby stabilizing the Fe3+ ions and preventing hydrolysis and precipitation that can occur at higher pH levels. Additionally, instead of using deionized water (as usual), all volumetric adjustments and dilutions were made with the 0. 0.1 mol/L HNO3 solution. This step was critical to maintain the same pH across all measurements and experimental conditions. By using a uniform acidic medium, we ensured that any variations in pH were minimized, thus providing reliable and reproducible results in the analysis of iron (III) solutions.

The results illustrated in Fig. 2 reveal that the PL intensity of CDs could be significantly quenched by Fe3+ and to a lesser extent by Al3+ and Pb2+, while Ni2+ caused a slight enhancing effect. All the other metal ions appear to have negligible effects on the CDs PL. One can state that, despite the synthesised CDs showed potential as a PL sensing platform for Fe3+, their lack of selectivity might compromise their utility, given that Al3+, Pb2+ and Ni2+ can also modulate the optical properties of the nanomaterial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL response of CDs to Fe3+, Al3+, Pb2+ and Ni2+
ce:section.ce:para: The next step involved assessing the PL properties of the carbon-based nanoparticle upon interacting with individual solutions of Fe3+, Pb2+, Al3+, and Ni2+ at increasing concentration levels of up to 0.5, 0.8, 0.6, and 0.8 mmol/L, respectively. The results clearly showed that the PL intensity of CD was gradually quenched when adding increasing concentrations of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Al3+, while a PL-enhancing effect was observed by increasing the concentration of Ni2+ (Fig. 3 ). In all cases, the PL response of CDs was metal ion concentration-dependent.

For the interaction process between CDs and Fe3+, Pb2+, and Al3+, the quenching effect was described by the Stern-Volmer equation (Fig. S1 – supplementary material). When CDs interacted with Fe3+ and Pb2+, a linear Stern-Volmer relationship was observed between F0/F and the concentration of the metal ion, for concentration values of up to 0.50 and 0.74 mmol/L for Fe3+ and Pb2+, respectively (Fig. S1 a) and b)). Regarding the interaction with Al3+, two distinct zones of linear Stern-Volmer response were identified (Fig. S1 c)): one exhibiting higher sensitivity to Al3+ of up to 0.30 mmol/L and another showing lower sensitivity within the concentration range of 0.32 to 0.60 mmol/L. Additionally, the analysis of the obtained Stern-Volmer equations indicates that CDs exhibited a much higher affinity for Fe3+ compared to Pb2+ and Al3+. In fact, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant obtained for Fe3+ (Ksv = 3.03(±0.08) L/mmol) was approximately 3 times higher than that obtained for Pb2+ (Ksv = 0.95(±0.05) L/mmol) and around 2 and 6 times higher than those obtained for Al3+ (Ksv = 1.5(±0.1) L/mmol and Ksv = 0.54(±0.07) L/mmol).

Concerning the interaction process between CDs and Ni2+, the enhancing effect was described by a Langmuir binding isotherm equation (Fig S1 d)) as follows:
Ni
2 +
FI
=
1
B ×
FI
max
+
1
F
I
max
×
Ni
2 +
in which B is the equilibrium binding constant, FI and FImax represent the fluorescence intensity in the presence of Ni2+ at a given concentration and the observed maximum intensity, respectively. The results revealed a good linearity between
Ni
2 +
FI
and
Ni
2 +
for Ni2+ concentration values of up to 0.74 mmol/L (R=0.9961). The equilibrium binding constant (B) of the Ni2+ on the surface of CDs was 2.66 × 106 L mmol−1. This constant value indeed demonstrates a high affinity of Ni2+ for the surface of CDs, as higher B values indicate stronger binding interactions in the context of the Langmuir binding isotherm. Therefore, the PL enhancement of CDs can be attributed to the incorporation of Ni2+ into the structure of CDs which can introduce additional electronic states and modify the surface properties, influencing the optical and luminescent characteristics of the CDs. For this reason, Ni2+ have been used as a doping agent to enhance the optical properties of CDs [41–43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the CDs PL quenching effect by Fe3+ and the other cations
ce:section.ce:para: The PL quenching mechanisms of the CDs upon interaction with the Fe3+ were assessed by recording the PL lifetime decay values of the CDs both alone (τ0) and with increasing concentrations (τ) of Fe3+ of up to 0.50 mmol/L. The findings depicted in Fig. 4 demonstrate that the PL lifetime remains practically unchanged upon the addition of increasing concentrations of Fe3+. Furthermore, the PL lifetime decay values of the CDs in the presence of other PL-quenching metal ions (Al3+ and Pb2+), each at a concentration of 0.80 mmol/L, remained unaffected, as illustrated in Fig. S2 (supplementary material).

This observation suggests that the quenching mechanism is predominantly governed by static processes, indicating the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex between the CDs and the PL-quenching species [44]. In situations involving static quenching, only fluorophores that have formed non-fluorescent complexes are affected, while the properties of the remaining fluorescent species remain unaltered [44].

These results are consistent with the PL quenching mechanism outlined in the scientific literature [36,37], which is attributed to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) processes. As abovementioned, the CDs have on their surface several functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and amine groups, which have the ability to chelate Fe3+ ions, forming stable complexes on the CDs’ surface. The functional groups of CDs act as electron donors, promoting the formation of coordinate bonds with Fe3+ ions. In the case of Al3+ and Pb2+, a similar quenching mechanism is expected to occur, albeit with a minor affinity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ determination using EEM and chemometric analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Previous results showed that the as-prepared CDs can be used as a PL sensing platform for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions; however, they do not exhibit a sufficient high selective behaviour towards to target ion. Under these conditions, the accurate determination of Fe3+ using univariate calibration methods can be compromised if the absence of interfering species such as Pb2+, Al3+, and Ni2+ is not guaranteed. To acquire second-order data and leverage the second-order advantage, mitigating unforeseen uncalibrated components that may arise in complex matrices, we investigated the acquisition of CDs EEM data either with Fe3+(Fig. 5 a) or without Fe3+ (Fig. 5b). The results illustrated in Fig. 5 depict spectral changes associated with the interaction with Fe3+ ions, revealing a noticeable quenching effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability evaluation of CDs through EEM data analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical significance of employing EEM arises only when there are no observable changes in the optical properties of the CDs over time, at a given wavelength. This implies that, upon the mixture of CDs and the analyte, the optical properties of the CDs undergo almost instantaneous alteration, remaining thereafter unchanged for extended periods without analytical relevance. Consequently, the stability in aqueous solution of the PL intensity of the CDs alone and in presence of Fe3+ was thoroughly evaluated. The PL intensity of CDs (λem = 436 nm), both individually and when mixed with 0.40 mmol/L of Fe3+, was monitored for 60 min using four different excitation wavelengths: 320, 340, 360 and 380 nm. The results (Fig. S3 – supplementary material) demonstrated that, regardless of exciting the CDs at 320, 340, 360, or 380 nm, the PL intensity of the probe, both in the absence and presence of 0.40 mmol/L of Fe3+, remains practically unchanged over 60 min. This underscores the notable stability exhibited over time for different excitation wavelengths, enabling the use of EEM data for quantification purposes. Hence, when dealing with second-order data, the chemometric model for data analysis chosen was unfolded partial least-squares (U-PLS) followed by residual bilinearization (RBL). This decision was based on its inherent second-order advantage and its recognized flexibility, establishing it as one of the most versatile linear second-order multivariate calibration techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multivariate calibration of Fe3+ using EEM data
ce:section.ce:para: After this preliminary optimization, it was important to establish the linear range for the quantification of Fe3+ using CDs, as well as determine the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ). In this context, several standards were prepared within a Fe3+ concentration range of 0.02 to 0.50 mmol/L. Through U-PLS modelling, a linear relation between EEMs data and Fe3+ was observed. The LOD and LOQ (calculated by averaging the respective maximum values obtained using the MVC2 interface) were determined to be 0.02 and 0.06 mmol/L, respectively. Therefore, the calibration curves were established within the range of 0.10 to 0.40 mmol/L of Fe3+ for further analysis. A typical calibration curve depicting the interaction of CDs and Fe3+ is shown in Fig. 6 .

As observed, the developed methodology proves to be suitable for the determination of Fe3+. After establishing the analytical parameters for Fe3+ determination, the next step involved determining the LOD and LOQ of each interfering species. This was crucial to prevent the complete quenching of CDs upon the addition of each interfering species while ensuring that the concentration of each interfering species remained above its respective LOD. These determinations were conducted for each interfering species individually. The respective calibration curves as well as the LOD are shown in Fig. 7 .

These results clearly highlight how the optical properties of CDs are influenced by all three metal ions (Pb2+, Al3+, and Ni2+), which are concentration-dependent. This dependence poses a challenge, potentially affecting the accurate and selective determination of Fe3+ in the presence of any of these metal ions. The study also demonstrated that utilizing U-PLS for the analysis of EEM data enables the quantification of each interfering species.
There are fewer standards in the calibration curve of Al3+ because, as abovementioned, this metal ion exhibited two distinct linear zones, and only the zone with the highest sensitivity was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective determination of Fe3+ in the absence and presence of each interfering species using RBL method
ce:section.ce:para:
After determining the working linear range for Fe3+ and the LOD for each interfering species, several experiments were carried out to assess the effectiveness of the RBL in overcoming the presence of uncalibrated species in second-order data. The results, as shown in Tables 1, 2 and 3
, demonstrate the effectiveness of RBL to selectively determine Fe3+ in the presence of Pb2+, Al3+ and Ni2+, respectively. Effectively, samples with three different concentration levels, in the absence and presence of the interfering species (also tested at two distinct concentrations), were analysed.

The analysis of results in Table 1 leads to the conclusion that for samples without Pb2+ (samples 1, 4 and 6), the RMSEP value remained consistently around 0.025 mmol/L, regardless of the application of RBL. Since these samples lack uncalibrated interfering species, the application of RBL does not enhance the quantification of Fe3+, as anticipated. Regarding the Pb2+ spiked samples (samples 2, 3, 5 and 7), employing 1 RBL resulted in a significant reduction of RMSEP when compared to that obtained without RBL application, from 0.0445 to 0.0103 mmol/L, respectively. This outcome is logical, considering that the addition of the uncalibrated species, Pb2+, to samples containing Fe3+ prompted the improvement. Additionally, employing 2 RBL components diminished the accuracy of the corresponding U-PLS model, leading to an increase in RMSEP values (0.0315 mmol/L), slightly lower to those obtained without the application of the RBL procedure (0.0445 mmol/L). In summary, the efficacy of the RBL procedure is demonstrated in the context of utilizing second-order data, highlighting its advantages.

In Table 2, the effectiveness of the application of RBL procedure is clearly evident. For samples without Al3+ (samples 1, 4 and 6), the RMSEP (0.0304 mmol/L) was approximately half of that obtained when applying 1 or 2 RBL components to these samples. This aligns with expectations since these samples do not contain any uncalibrated interfering species, therefore, the application of RBL does not improve the quantification of Fe3+. However, in samples spiked with Al3+ (samples 2, 3, 5 and 7), the application of 1 RBL reduced the RMSEP to values approximately four times lower. This makes sense, as one uncalibrated species, namely Al3+, was added to samples containing Fe3+. Moreover, the application of 2 RBL decreased the accuracy of the respective U-PLS model, as the RMSEP increased to values (0.0730 mmol/L) similar to those obtained without the application of RBL procedure (0.0813 mmol/L). The results provide additional confirmation of the RBL procedure's efficacy, reinforcing the benefits of incorporating second-order data in the analysis.

In Table 3, the samples without Ni2+ (samples 1, 4 and 6) showed a lower RMSEP value (0.0335 mmol/L) compared to those obtained when applying 1 or 2 RBL components. Although the difference was not as pronounced as observed in samples with Al3+, it remained noticeable. As anticipated, this result is in accordance with what was expected as these samples do not contain any uncalibrated interfering species, consistent with the earlier mentioned rationale. For samples containing Ni2+ (samples 2, 3, 5 and 7), once again, the application of 1 RBL resulted in a lowest RMSEP value (0.0252 mmol/L) in contrast to values obtained without RBL (0.0344 mmol/L) or with the application of 2 RBL components (0.0840 mmol/L). This consistency makes sense, as only one uncalibrated species, namely Ni2+, has been added to samples containing Fe3+. These outcomes once again highlight the efficacy of the RBL procedure, underscoring the benefits of integrating second-order data in the analysis.

The efficacy of the RBL procedure was more pronounced in samples spiked with uncalibrated AL3+, probably because this metal ion exhibited higher reactivity towards CDs than Pb2+ and Ni2+, as evident in Fig. 2. The determination of the optimal number of RBL components can be guided by prior knowledge of the system. In this study, where only one uncalibrated interfering species was introduced, the optimal number of RBL components should be 1 [45]. In cases where prior knowledge about the system composition, specifically the number of interfering species, is absent, the optimal number of RBL components can be determined by comparing the final residues values of the test samples with those of the instrumental noise. Therefore, the optimal number of RBL components is identified when both values are similar [34].

This manuscript underscores the advantage of using second-order data, owing to the second-order advantage, for the quantification of a target parameter/property in the presence of uncalibrated species – a situation frequently encountered in real life samples where avoiding the presence of uncalibrated species is often impossible.

This study highlights the effectiveness of applying the U-PLS/RBL chemometric model to process fluorescence data, specifically EEMs known as second-order data. This approach enables the accurate quantification of Fe(III) in samples containing uncalibrated and interfering species, including Pb(II), Al(III), and Ni(II), without the need for pre-treatment.

In fact, the RMSEP decreased significantly after the application of one RBL component in the samples spiked with each of the uncalibrated and interfering species, namely around four times lower for Al(III) and Pb(II). Interestingly, applying one RBL component to samples containing only Fe(III) (samples without spiking) did not reduce the RMSEP values; instead, there was an increase, especially for Al(III) and Ni(II). This means that for samples containing only Fe(III) without any additional interfering species, accurate determination of the target metal ion can be achieved solely through the application of the U-PLS model. However, in samples spiked with interfering species, individually, the selective and accurate quantification of ferric ion requires the application of the RBL post-calibration procedure.

Moreover, the metal ions used as uncalibrated and interfering species in this study exhibited a strong interaction with the CDs fluorescence probe, enhancing the efficacy of the RBL procedure and emphasizing the advantages of using second-order data.

This study marks the first example where second-order data, specifically through the use of the RBL procedure, has successfully overcome the presence of interfering species in QDs-based methodologies. The application of RBL significantly improved accuracy, reducing RMSEP values, particularly in samples spiked with Al(III) and Pb(II). This breakthrough demonstrates the potential for future analytical methods to leverage the advantages of chemometric tools like U-PLS/RBL, facilitating accurate quantification in complex samples without the need for labour-intensive pre-treatment processes. This versatile and straightforward approach can be extended to various complex samples and different analytes, paving the way for more efficient and accurate analytical applications. Additionally, while this study shows a good effect on iron ion detection, it is suggested that this technology could also be feasible for detecting other ions in the future. By using this or other types of QDs as probes, the method's applicability and impact can be further extended, enhancing its relevance for diverse analytical challenges.

The developed manuscript describes a straightforward, accurate and versatile method for the quantification of metal species in the presence of uncalibrated species, eliminating the need for laborious and time-consuming removal procedures. This approach utilizes CDs, which are biocompatible and exhibit low cytotoxicity, thereby expanding the application range.
This work received financial support from FCT/MCTES (UIDB/50006/2020 DOI https://doi.org/10.54499/UIDB/50006/2020) through national funds.

Santos: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. David S.M. Ribeiro: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111207.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
